text: to see something intriguing every time he passed through the Market ? “I just saw you dancing. I’m willing to trade .” “I don’t dance for bots anymore!” She raised the bar again and took a step forward, ready to swing. “And you have no damned right to spy on me !” Ronin didn’t move. “You left the door open .” “That is not an invitation .” He dipped a hand into his pocket, closing it around the canteen. The woman stiffened, face paling . “What are you doing?” Her voice wavered with uncertainty . Slowly, he withdrew the container and held it up for her to see. Then he leaned across the threshold and placed it on the ground . “What is that ?” “Holds liquid,” he replied. “You know. Water, oil, gasoline. Dust, if you want .” “I don’t trade with bots.” She stared at the canteen . “Just for a dance .” “You already saw one .” Ronin considered her statement. She was right. Nothing was free in this world. And the canteen had some value; it could be traded for a few rounds of ammunition, or leather to repair his boots. There’d be someone in town interested in obtaining it who had something worth Ronin’s while . “Then it is payment for the first. What do you want for another ?” “I told you, I don’t trade with bots. Take it and get out .” Her expression was at odds with her tone, her inner conflict clear on her face . “My name is Ronin. What is yours ?” “Your metal skull that dense that you don’t understand what I said? Fuck off !” He’d done her no wrong — not that he could perceive, anyway — and had never seen her before. He couldn’t guess why she would treat him in such a manner, but it didn’t matter. He was wasting time. If he pushed her to violence, his defensive programming was likely to kick in. It would be too easy to end her, and, despite her hostility, he had no desire to harm this woman . She’d mentioned spying. He reminded himself that humans were particular about having their own, private spaces. Ronin had intruded upon her security . He ran his optics over her a final time. Her shoulders rose and fell with her heavy breaths, the steel bar hovering closer to her waistline now. But there was more to her, something he hadn’t given much attention to before — her imperfections . Those freckles, the light scar on her wrist, the thinness of her frame. Her fingernails were dirty and broken, her hands rough, face smudged with dirt. Synths and humans were virtually identical on the surface. Damage to a synth would alter its appearance slightly, but humans changed so much, so often. They wore their hardships on their bodies, told their stories through their scars. For a bot, damage was often fleeting. Cracked casings could be sealed, burned-out circuits replaced, actuators repaired, synthetic skin refabricated . A synth could be made to look new again. A human, for good or ill, could not . He turned and stepped onto the road . “I said to take your bottle!” she shouted . Ronin didn’t answer. Shrouded in the shadows cast by the Bot District’s electric glow, he headed for the Market without looking back . He replayed the woman’s dance on a loop as he walked, seeking new ways to analyze it, to reduce it to mathematics and discern the obscure, underlying pattern that would unlock true understanding. It was almost as difficult as puzzling out the woman’s mood . Disdain between bots and humans was not uncommon, but she’d displayed something more intense. Despite their physical shortcomings, humans were psychologically complex creatures, and their way of viewing the world was beyond most bots’ ability to comprehend — but just barely . He passed through the open gates and into the Market. It was contained within a large, separate section of the wall, five hundred feet by seven hundred. Eleven pre-Black Out buildings stood within, though it wasn’t likely that their original purposes had survived. Closer to the gate, a patch of cracked asphalt hosted a variety of stalls from which vendors sold their wares . The exposed metal of Ronin’s hands gleamed beneath the white flood lights. He scanned the crowd, easily picking out the humans because of their wavering postures and the sheens of sweat on their imperfect skin . Most of the humans in Cheyenne seemed to return to their homes before the sun set, making the few organics here now the minority. They were largely gathered at the food vendor’s booth, haggling over meals as pots steamed behind the counter. The cook was a bot, a sleek white and red model with basic facial features that only vaguely approximated those of a synth or human. Everyone was made in the image of the Creators, but none had been made equally . Ronin shifted his optics to Kitty’s. The lights on the outside were garish; pink, purple, and blue neon that had somehow weathered the Black Out and now served to lure bots and humans alike into a place where a variety of pleasures could — for a price — be sampled. It made him think of the redhaired human once again . How would she have danced, had she accepted his proposal? He could simulate millions of possibilities, if he chose, but didn’t bother wasting the time or the energy. His processors would never match the reality of her movement . During his time in Cheyenne, he’d never once entered Kitty’s. Hadn’t, in fact, had a woman — metal or meat — in 4,112 days, since long before coming into town . Dancers! Exclaimed one of the signs. Perhaps the redhaired woman was right. He could find what he wanted at Kitty’s, after he saw to his salvage . The scrapper, Zeke, operated out of a large building
